why: I wrote "Why" last, even though it opens the record. I'd finished sixteen other chapters and realized none of them answered the question that had been sitting underneath all of it. I didn't want a tidy answer. I wanted the question itself to be the song — repeated, turned over, never quite resolved. The piano part is almost embarrassingly simple. I kept stripping it back every time I tried to make it more impressive, because the simplicity felt closer to how the actual question sits in a person's chest.
who-wins-if-i-win: This one came out of a conversation I had at my grandfather's funeral, of all places. Someone mentioned how hard he'd worked, how much he'd achieved, and I remember thinking: achieved for who? He missed so much of his own children's lives chasing that achievement. The title is the question I couldn't stop asking myself afterward, about my own choices.
the-roots-we-cannot-see: I think about inheritance a lot — not money, but the quieter things that get planted in you before you're old enough to notice. This song is about my grandmother, mostly, though I never name her. She gave me things I didn't recognize as gifts until decades later. The strings in the bridge are meant to feel like something underground, finally surfacing.
if-we-cant-say-the-hard-truths: This is the angriest song on the record, even though it doesn't sound angry. I was thinking about every family dinner where something real went unsaid because it was easier to keep things pleasant. The silence in this song is doing as much work as the lyrics.
still-worth-it: I almost cut this one. It felt too plain next to the others. But a friend convinced me that the plainness was the point — some of the truest things we say to ourselves don't come with elaborate language. "Still worth it" is a sentence I've had to say to myself more times than I'd like to admit.
side-by-side: This is the closest thing to a love song on the record, though it's not really about romance. It's about the specific, undramatic comfort of doing ordinary things next to someone you trust completely. I wanted the guitar and piano to feel like they were having that same kind of quiet conversation.
as-long-as-youre-okay: Written for a friend going through something I couldn't fix. I learned, writing this, that sometimes the most honest thing you can offer someone isn't a solution — it's just presence, repeated, unconditional. The chorus says the same four words almost the entire song because that's genuinely all I had to offer.
it-was-already-there: This is the heart of the record for me. I spent years — actual years — looking outward for whatever I thought was missing. The day I finally understood that the missing piece had been with me the whole time, just unrecognized, was disorienting in a way I still haven't fully processed. The strings come in late and quiet, the way that understanding actually arrived for me.
always-in-your-corner: My brother and I don't say "I love you" easily. This song is the closest I've gotten to saying it to him directly, dressed up in melody so neither of us has to be embarrassed about it.
the-house-we-couldnt-leave: Houses hold things. This one is about the family home none of us could quite let go of, even after it stopped making practical sense to keep it. The choir voices in the second half are meant to feel like the house itself, finally speaking.
i-never-knew-any-other-way: About growing up inside a way of loving that I didn't realize was unusual until I left it and saw other families up close. Not better or worse — just different, and I didn't have the language for that difference until much later.
leave-a-light-on: My mother kept the kitchen light on every night I came home late, for years, without ever mentioning it. I didn't understand what it meant until I wrote this song, honestly. Some gestures only become legible to you decades after they were given.
the-empty-chair: This one is about grief that has finally found a place to sit, instead of constantly disrupting everything around it. I wanted the arrangement to feel settled, almost peaceful, even though the subject is loss — because that's eventually what real grief becomes, if you let it.
good-things-grow-slow: I'm not a patient person by nature, so this song was partly me trying to talk myself into a virtue I didn't actually have yet. Writing it slowly, over months instead of days, was its own small practice in the thing it's about.
maybe-i-was-wrong: The hardest four words I know how to sing. This song took longer to record than any other on the album because I kept flinching on the actual line. There's a take where my voice cracks slightly on "wrong" that we kept, on purpose, instead of fixing it.
borrowed-time: Written after a health scare in the family that turned out, thankfully, to be manageable — but for about a week, we didn't know that. This song is about the strange, unearned gratitude that arrives once you've briefly stared at the alternative.
free-men-tell-the-truth: The closing chapter, and the one I argued with myself about most. It's about the cost of honesty and the cost of silence, and which one is actually heavier in the long run. I wanted the album to end on a question of freedom, not an answer — because I think that's where the whole record actually lives.
you-were-never-broken: This is the thesis statement for the whole record, and I almost didn't put it first because it felt too on-the-nose. But every time I tried opening with something subtler, the album lost its spine. I wanted the first thing anyone hears to be a direct address — not a metaphor to decode, just a sentence aimed straight at whoever needed to hear it that day.
the-weight-that-taught-you: Written about the specific kind of strength nobody asks for — the one you build by surviving something, not by training for it. I kept the low end heavy on purpose in this one. I wanted the bass to feel like the actual weight being described, not just illustrate it.
start-somewhere: I rewrote the second verse four times because it kept sounding like advice, and I didn't want to give advice — I wanted to describe the specific paralysis of having too many unfinished starts behind you. The version that stuck is the most honest, least helpful one. It doesn't tell you how to start. It just tells you that starting badly still counts.
fear-was-never-the-point: This came out of noticing how much of my own life I'd spent managing fear instead of pursuing whatever the fear was standing in front of. The title is something I had to say to myself in the mirror more than once before I trusted it enough to put it in a song.
the-version-that-survived: About the self you become after the version that couldn't make it through gets quietly retired. I didn't want this to sound triumphant — survival usually doesn't feel like a victory lap while it's happening. The vocal take we kept is a little tired-sounding on purpose.
nothing-needs-fixing-tonight: Written on an actual night when I couldn't sleep trying to solve problems that weren't solvable at 2am. The whole song is basically me talking myself down. I left a long, unhurried instrumental section near the end because some nights the only fix is just waiting for morning.
the-work-nobody-sees: For everyone doing the unglamorous, uncredited labor that makes everything else possible. I thought about my own early years a lot writing this — the unpaid, unnoticed work that nobody puts on a resume but that actually built whatever came after.
permission: The shortest title on the record and the hardest one to earn. This song took the longest to finish because I kept adding things to justify the permission instead of just granting it. The final version is sparser than every draft before it — I cut almost everything that sounded like an argument for why you're allowed.
blood-on-the-hallelujah: I wanted a praise song that didn't pretend the praising was easy. The take we kept has the vocalist's voice genuinely fraying near the end of the bridge — we could have fixed it in a cleaner pass, but the fraying was the whole point. Some gratitude costs something to say out loud.
carry-you-home: Written late one night after helping a friend through something I couldn't actually fix, just be present for. The bassline does most of the emotional work here — it never resolves cleanly, just keeps walking, the way you do when you're holding someone up.
its-not-always-easy: The most understated title on the album and, I think, the most honest. I avoided every temptation to add a triumphant key change at the end. Some things just stay hard, and the song needed to be willing to end without resolving that.
keep-a-chair-for-you: About the people we keep space for, whether or not they ever come back to fill it. I recorded the piano part with one chair deliberately left empty in the booth — superstitious, maybe, but it helped me play it the way I wanted it to feel.
mercy-wears-a-black-coat: I like writing about grace that doesn't look like grace at first. This one's about the people who help you in ways that initially feel like punishment. The arrangement is colder than most of the record on purpose — mercy isn't always warm when it arrives.
the-heart-comes-home-at-night: Most of this was written between midnight and 3am, which felt appropriate given the subject. There's a specific quiet that only exists at that hour, and I tried to build the whole arrangement around protecting that quiet rather than filling it.
the-river-knows-your-name: About the places that remember us even when we've stopped consciously remembering them. The recurring melodic phrase in this one is meant to feel like something returning, not something new — like the river finding the same bend it's always found.
the-truth-has-teeth: I wrote this after a hard conversation with someone I love, where the honest thing and the kind thing weren't the same thing for once. The horn section bites a little on the choruses — that wasn't an accident.
the-woman-beside-the-fire: A tribute to the people who do quiet, unglamorous, essential work and never ask for credit. I thought of my own grandmother, and of every person like her, while writing it. The warmth in the arrangement is meant to be felt, not announced.
noiraciel-angel-of-darkness: The opening track of the whole metal record, and the one that took longest to get right because I kept trying to make the darkness sound evil instead of simply powerful. Once I stopped moralizing it and just let the riff be strong, the song finally clicked.
noiraciel-ashes-of-heaven: About what's left standing after something sacred burns down — and the strange discovery that ash is fertile, not just empty. The guitar tone here is deliberately scorched-sounding, run through a chain I usually avoid because it's almost too harsh. Almost.
noiraciel-black-wings-rising: I wrote this about the unbearable instant between who you were and who you're about to become. The tempo shift two-thirds through is meant to feel like that exact threshold — the held breath right before the jump.
noiraciel-blind-halo: About grace that doesn't recognize itself as grace. This one started as a much angrier song and softened as I rewrote it, until what was left was less about fury and more about a light that doesn't know it's shining.
noiraciel-blood-on-the-halo: Inspired by the idea of kintsugi — repairing broken pottery with gold instead of hiding the crack. I wanted the production to do that literally: the vocal has visible damage in it that we highlighted instead of fixing.
noiraciel-broken-wings-burning-soul: About falling as the unglamorous middle of flight, not the opposite of it. The drum pattern keeps stumbling and catching itself, on purpose — it's not supposed to sound graceful. It's supposed to sound like learning.
noiraciel-crown-of-fire: This is about earned authority versus inherited authority, and I'll admit it's partly about my own insecurities around legitimacy — feeling like I had to prove I belonged in rooms I was already in. The vocal delivery is deliberately unpolished in the verses and full-throated in the chorus, the difference between doubt and the moment you finally claim something.
noiraciel-darkness-made-divine: About finding the sacred in the places everyone else calls empty. I recorded the quietest sections of this track in an actual dark room with the lights off, which sounds dramatic, but it changed how I sang it.
noiraciel-fallen-without-fear: About choosing to fall instead of being pushed — a very different feeling, even though it looks the same from the outside. This is one of the more personal songs on the record, about leaving something stable on purpose.
noiraciel-heaven-burns-tonight: I wanted a song that felt like the whole sky catching fire — every value tested at once. The wall of sound in the chorus is intentionally overwhelming. It's not meant to be comfortable to listen to the first time.
noiraciel-mercy-in-flames: About forgiveness that genuinely costs the person giving it something, rather than the easy, free kind. I kept the melody warmer than the lyrical content might suggest — mercy, even when it's hard-won, should still sound like mercy.
noiraciel-no-light-left: Written from the bottom — the place with nothing left to lose, and therefore, strangely, nothing left to fear. This is the most minimal arrangement on the record. I kept removing instruments until what was left actually matched the emptiness in the lyric.
noiraciel-saint-of-the-damned: About staying when everyone else has left — being the witness nobody else volunteered to be. I wrote this thinking about people who do thankless work for audiences of almost no one, and decided that was worth a hymn.
noiraciel-sin-of-an-angel: About the choice that makes us human — breaking a rule on purpose because some deeper truth demanded it. I left the bridge melodically unresolved because the choice it's describing doesn't really resolve either.
noiraciel-the-devil-knows-my-name: About being fully known, even at your worst, and discovering that isn't the end of you. This took several rewrites because early drafts were too afraid of the subject matter to actually say anything. The final version doesn't flinch.
noiraciel-when-angels-go-to-war: About righteous anger that doesn't apologize for its own fire. I wanted the gentlest voice on the record to deliver the fiercest lyric, because I think that contrast is closer to how real courage usually arrives — quietly, until it isn't quiet anymore.
noiraciel-the-last-prayer: The closing track of the metal record, and the one where I finally let myself say something plainly instead of through metaphor. It's about the last honest word, spoken when there's no more time to dress it up. I tried to sing it like I meant it, because I did.
the-velvet-machine: The title track, and the concept that holds the whole album together — the city as a beautiful, indifferent machine, and the small human warmth that survives inside it anyway. I built the production around that contrast: cold synths underneath, but a warm vocal sitting right on top of them, refusing to be processed away.
a-body-made-of-night: About arriving somewhere new and feeling like you're being assembled out of the city's spare parts before you've had a chance to define yourself. The synth bass is deliberately mechanical-sounding. The vocal is the only organic thing fighting against it.
accordion-heart: I wanted an instrument that felt handmade in a song otherwise full of machines, so the accordion carries almost all the emotional weight here. It's the one part of the arrangement that breathes unevenly, like an actual person.
the-saints-go-clubbing: The most playful song on a fairly heavy record. I wanted something that let the sacred and the nightlife sit in the same room without judging each other. The bassline is doing most of the smiling.
she-dances-like-a-memory: About someone who exists more vividly in recollection than they ever did in the room. The vocal is intentionally a little blurred in the mix, like trying to remember a face you haven't seen in years.
lisbon-is-not-a-city-it-is-a-wound: The most personal song on this record. I won't say much more than that, except that some places hold grief the way a body holds a scar — visible, healed over, but still there if you know where to look.
contra-bass-confession: Written almost entirely around the low end, on purpose — confession felt like it needed to come from somewhere deep and unglamorous, not from a pretty top line. The bass is basically the narrator of this song.
hard-techno-prayer: About finding something like devotion on a dance floor at 4am, which sounds like a joke until it happens to you. I genuinely treated the arrangement like a hymn with a kick drum instead of an organ.
the-girl-with-the-cello-scar: About carrying a visible mark from something that also gave you your gift. The cello part is intentionally a little raw-sounding — I didn't want it polished smooth, because the scar isn't smooth either.
no-one-leaves-the-night-unchanged: I wanted the production to feel like the actual passage of one long night — it builds and recedes in waves rather than following a normal song structure. Some sections don't repeat at all, because that night doesn't either.
saxophone-for-the-damned-and-the-loved: The saxophone in this one had to do double duty — sounding mournful and celebratory in the same phrase, because I think that's true of a lot of nightlife music if you listen closely enough.
the-future-has-an-accent: About migration and the specific music that travels in a person's voice no matter where they end up. I deliberately layered instruments from different traditions on top of the electronic base, refusing to let any one of them dominate.
velvet-bruises: About softness that still carries marks — the idea that gentleness and damage aren't opposites. The production is lush and warm on the surface; listen underneath it and there's something a little more fragile holding it up.
when-the-machines-learn-saudade: I wanted to ask what happens when something built to be efficient accidentally learns to long for something. The synths in the bridge are deliberately a little 'wrong' — slightly out of tune, like a machine encountering a feeling it wasn't designed to have.
still-human-after-all-this-noise: The closing track of the record, and the answer to everything the album had been asking — that underneath all the machinery, the noise, the city's indifference, something stubbornly human keeps surviving. I let the final chorus get messier and more live-sounding than anything else on the album, on purpose. Humanity isn't clean.
still-we-sail: The title track and the whole album's promise — that even after loss, even mid-crossing, the boat keeps moving. I wanted the opening to feel less like a beginning and more like something already in motion when we arrive, because grief rarely waits for a clean starting point.
exile-body: About carrying displacement in the body itself, not just in circumstance. The vocal phrasing is intentionally a little unsettled, never quite landing on the beat the way you'd expect — that unsettledness is the point.
the-accordion-breathes: I wanted an instrument that physically breathes to carry a song about breath and survival. The accordion part was recorded almost without editing, including the audible inhales, because removing them would have removed the whole metaphor.
letters-never-crossed: About words that got written but never sent, or sent but never answered. The arrangement has call-and-response phrases that deliberately never quite meet each other.
salt-gospel: About finding the sacred in something as plain as the sea and the work it demands of the people who live by it. I wrote this thinking of fishing communities, and tried to give the chorus the weight of an actual hymn.
she-who-stayed-behind: Not every story is about the one who leaves. This is for the ones who stay, who hold the place steady so there's something to return to. The arrangement stays grounded and doesn't chase the drama the leaving songs get to have.
atlantic-nocturne: Written at night, about the ocean at night, which sounds obvious but the timing mattered — I wanted the particular quality of an ocean you can hear but not see. The mix is deliberately darker and more reverberant than the rest of the record.
grief-in-different-latitudes: About how mourning changes shape depending on where you're standing when it catches you. I layered instruments associated with different places on top of each other without resolving them into one identity, because grief experienced away from home doesn't resolve neatly either.
the-port-we-cannot-find: About searching for a destination that may not exist in the form you're imagining it. The song never arrives at a clear final chord — it just fades, mid-search, because that felt more honest than a false arrival.
what-fado-holds: An homage to the Portuguese tradition of fado and the specific, untranslatable ache it carries — saudade. I tried to write something that respected the tradition without imitating it note for note. It's my own attempt at holding that same kind of longing.
saudade-has-a-weight: A direct continuation of the feeling in the previous track, but heavier, more physical. I wanted this one to feel like something you carry in your chest, not just an emotion you name.
all-the-water-between: About distance — geographic, emotional, the kind that separates people who still love each other across an ocean. The instrumentation deliberately spreads wide in the stereo field, like the water itself is taking up space between two things that belong together.
the-sea-keeps-our-names: About places that remember the people who passed through them long after those people are gone. This was one of the more emotional ones to record — there's a vocal take we kept specifically because it cracked slightly on the word "names."
i-chose-the-open: About choosing the harder, less certain path on purpose, because the safe one had stopped being honest. The production opens up significantly in the second half — more space, fewer walls — to mirror that choice.
still-we-sail-to-light: The closing track, and the album's answer to its own question — yes, even now, even after everything this record has carried, we keep sailing, and there's light at the end of it. I let the arrangement brighten gradually across the whole song, so the hope isn't sudden. It's earned, slowly, the way real hope usually is.
